title: Invisible Cycles: Characterizing and Quantifying CPU Time Displacement from Asynchronous Kernel Execution in Modern Linux
description: Modern Linux kernels increasingly execute work asynchronously on behalf of user processes — through io_uring worker threads, softirq handlers, workqueues, and eBPF programs — but this displaced CPU time is not attributed to the originating process by the scheduler. We formally characterize this CPU time displacement phenomenon, derive analytical bounds on the resulting scheduler fairness violations under EEVDF, and propose Causal Charge Propagation (CCP), a cost-attribution mechanism that restores fairness guarantees.
motivation: As io_uring, eBPF, and other async kernel mechanisms become the dominant I/O and processing paths in datacenter workloads, an increasing fraction of CPU time escapes the scheduler's accounting. This undermines EEVDF's fairness guarantees, breaks cgroup resource isolation (critical for container orchestration), and causes systematic errors in CPU billing for cloud tenants. The problem is recognized by practitioners (Kubernetes issue #125409, liburing issue #916) but has never been formally characterized or quantified.
proposed_approach: We take a four-pronged approach: (1) Catalog all async execution mechanisms in the Linux kernel and characterize their displacement properties. (2) Develop a queueing-theoretic model that formalizes CPU time displacement and derives closed-form bounds on fairness violation as a function of displacement intensity and heterogeneity. (3) Build a discrete-event simulator of the EEVDF scheduler augmented with async relay mechanisms to validate analytical bounds under diverse workload scenarios. (4) Design and evaluate Causal Charge Propagation (CCP), a mechanism that tracks work across async boundaries and propagates CPU charges back to originating processes.

related_work.title: A Proportional Share Resource Allocation Algorithm for Real-Time, Time-Shared Systems
related_work.relation: Foundational EEVDF algorithm; we extend its fairness analysis to account for displaced CPU time

related_work.title: ghOSt: Fast & Flexible User-Space Delegation of Linux Scheduling
related_work.relation: Delegates scheduling to userspace for flexibility; does not address async CPU time attribution

related_work.title: Mitigating Context Switching in Densely Packed Linux Clusters with Latency-Aware Group Scheduling
related_work.relation: Studies EEVDF under container workloads but focuses on context switching, not displacement

related_work.title: io_uring for High-Performance DBMSs: When and How to Use It
related_work.authors: Jasny, El-Hindi, Ziegler, Leis, and Binnig
related_work.relation: Evaluates io_uring performance for databases; does not study scheduling fairness implications

related_work.title: Performance Isolation and Fairness for Multi-Tenant Cloud Storage
related_work.relation: Studies I/O fairness in multi-tenant storage; our work extends fairness analysis to CPU scheduling under async execution

related_work.title: Shenango: Achieving High CPU Efficiency for Latency-sensitive Datacenter Workloads
related_work.relation: Optimizes CPU efficiency via fast core reallocation; complementary to our accounting-focused approach

related_work.title: Tales of the Tail: Hardware, OS, and Application-level Sources of Tail Latency
related_work.relation: Identifies OS-level sources of tail latency including scheduling artifacts; our work identifies a new source: displaced CPU time

related_work.title: Achieving Microsecond-Scale Tail Latency Efficiently with Approximate Optimal Scheduling
related_work.relation: Optimizes tail latency via near-optimal scheduling; our accounting mechanism is complementary

hypothesis: CPU time displacement from asynchronous kernel execution accounts for 10-40% of total CPU consumption in I/O-intensive workloads, creates measurable fairness violations (Jain's index dropping below 0.9 with 8+ competing processes under heterogeneous displacement), and can be mitigated by Causal Charge Propagation to within 5% of ideal fairness at less than 3% overhead.
success_criteria: Displacement accounts for at least 10% of total CPU time in at least one representative workload class
Jain's fairness index on effective CPU shares drops below 0.9 under heterogeneous displacement with 8+ competing processes
CCP restores fairness index to at least 0.95 with at most 5% overhead for at least one propagation strategy
Analytical bounds from the formal model match simulation results within 15%

computational_requirements.methodology: Analytical modeling + discrete-event simulation in Python; no special hardware needed
